steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence says NATO's collective defense gives Canada 'both benefits and [blank].' What follows — 'the organization's many members... are all bound to defend Canada, the reverse is also true' — elaborates on what those obligations look like in practice. A colon is the correct mark to introduce a clause that directly explains or expands on the preceding noun.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — obligations: while the: The colon correctly introduces the elaborating clause, and 'while' sets up the contrast ('bound to defend Canada... the reverse is also true'). ✓
  • Choice B — obligations; the: A semicolon links two independent clauses of equal weight; here the second clause elaborates on 'obligations,' making a colon the better choice. Also drops the contrast word 'while.' ✗
  • Choice C — obligations, while the: A comma is too weak to introduce a full explanatory clause after the summary phrase 'both benefits and obligations.' ✗
  • Choice D — obligations. The: A period cuts the sentence off and severs the direct link between 'obligations' and its explanation. ✗
That matches Choice A — obligations: while the.
